metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the new sidecar field that carries the review-time code SHA (e.g. `verified_at_sha`), specify it as a required addition to `sidecar.build_payload`, and describe the accessor the new sidecar-path branch of `remediation_mode_candidate` uses to read it — so the emit path and the read path are unambiguously coupled.
evidence: sidecar.py build_payload (lines 428-455): payload keys are schema, impact_model_version, verdict, ticket_id, ticket_type, material_fingerprint, model, runner, metrics, coverage, findings, coaching — no SHA field of any kind.
Plan phrase: 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present' — the hedge 'if not already present' implies conditional work, but the field is unconditionally absent.

attest.py line 246: the current code_unchanged check reads `signing.verified_at_sha_from_manifest(manifest)` from the cryptographic manifest. The plan's sidecar fallback path needs an equivalent reader for the sidecar payload, but neither the field name nor the accessor function is named in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside [material_fingerprint] if not already present,' implying the field may already exist in the sidecar. A read of sidecar.py's build_payload confirms the field is definitively absent — there is no verified_at_sha, code_sha, or any SHA field in the sidecar payload schema. More critically, the plan never names the new sidecar field or specifies how the new fallback branch of remediation_mode_candidate will read the code SHA from a sidecar payload (vs. reading it from the cryptographic manifest via signing.verified_at_sha_from_manifest). This leaves the implementer to invent a field name and a read accessor with no spec-level contract.
findings.location: What section: 'baseline material fingerprint and reviewed-code SHA come from the most recent sidecar (material_fingerprint is already persisted; persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present)'
scenarios: Implementer must invent a sidecar field name (e.g. 'verified_at_sha' vs 'code_sha') with no spec guidance, risking a mismatch between the emit path and the read path in remediation_mode_candidate.

If the field is named differently from what the new remediation_mode_candidate branch expects, the code_unchanged precondition will always be False on the sidecar path, silently defeating the feature.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/Scope section: 'Add `verified_at_sha: str | None` to `sidecar.build_payload` (populated from `current_code_sha()` at emit time). The sidecar-path `code_unchanged` check in `remediation_mode_candidate` reads `prior_sidecar_payload.get("verified_at_sha")` and compares it to `current_code_sha()`; both None → treated as changed (fail-safe).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `registry_version` must also be persisted to the sidecar payload (alongside the new code SHA field) so the sidecar-path `registry_unchanged` precondition has a valid baseline to compare against; update the 'sidecar field addition if needed' scope note accordingly.
evidence: sidecar.py build_payload (lines 428-455): returns schema, material_fingerprint, model, runner, metrics, coverage, findings, coaching — no registry_version/regver field
attest.py remediation_mode_candidate (lines 214-221): reasons dict includes 'registry_unchanged' as a required precondition

Plan What section: 'baseline material fingerprint and reviewed-code SHA come from the most recent sidecar (material_fingerprint is already persisted; persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present)' — no mention of registry_version
ABSENCE: no mention of persisting registry_version to the sidecar anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The `registry_unchanged` precondition in the new sidecar-fallback path requires reading the `registry_version` that was in effect when the prior review ran, but the current `build_payload` in `sidecar.py` does NOT persist `registry_version` — only `material_fingerprint` is stored today. The plan explicitly mentions adding only the code SHA ('persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present') and is silent on also persisting `registry_version`. Without it, the implementer has no baseline to evaluate `registry_unchanged` against on the sidecar path, forcing a choice between silently skipping the check (weakening the gate) or always treating it as 'changed' (defeating BLOCK-loop eligibility).
findings.location: What section ('persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present') and Scope section ('sidecar field addition if needed')
scenarios: Implementer adds only code SHA to sidecar, then cannot evaluate registry_unchanged from the sidecar path — either silently returns True (weakens the gate) or returns False always (BLOCK loops remain ineligible).
A BLOCK-loop ticket where the registry changed between rounds incorrectly becomes eligible for remediation mode because the sidecar-path has no baseline regver to compare against.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, extend 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present' to also cover registry_version: 'persist the review-time code SHA and registry_version alongside material_fingerprint if not already present'. In sidecar.py build_payload, add `"registry_version": registry_version(repo_root)` (import from manifest) alongside the new `"code_sha"` field. The sidecar-path branch of remediation_mode_candidate then reads `prior.get("registry_version")` for the `registry_unchanged` comparison, treating a missing field as `None` (conservative: `registry_unchanged = False`) so legacy sidecars without the field remain ineligible until a new sidecar is written.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the new sidecar field for the review-time code SHA (e.g. `verified_at_sha`), specify whether it is added to the existing `plan_review_result_v2` payload as an optional field (preserving the schema guard) or triggers a `_v3` bump, and add its definition to `sidecar.build_payload`'s field-selection block.
evidence: Plan text: 'baseline material fingerprint and reviewed-code SHA come from the most recent sidecar (material_fingerprint is already persisted; persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present)'

sidecar.build_payload (lines 428–455) returns a dict with keys: schema, impact_model_version, verdict, ticket_id, ticket_type, material_fingerprint, model, runner, metrics, coverage, findings, coaching — NO code SHA field present
ABSENCE: no field name ('code_sha', 'verified_at_sha', 'review_code_sha', etc.) is ever named in the plan
findings.finding: The new sidecar field for the review-time code SHA — needed by the sidecar-fallback `code_unchanged` precondition — has no defined field name, payload placement, or schema-version impact. `sidecar.build_payload` (confirmed by reading the full function) does not currently persist any code SHA; the plan says 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present' but never names the field or states whether this bumps the schema to v3 or adds an optional v2 field.
findings.location: ## What — 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present'; sidecar.py::build_payload
scenarios: An implementer must invent a field name; two independent implementations pick different names, breaking readers of the sidecar fallback path.

A schema version bump (v2→v3) vs. an optional v2 field changes the schema guard in latest_review_result, which only accepts 'plan_review_result_v1'/'plan_review_result_v2' — a v3 would be silently skipped.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'verified_at_sha': verdict.get('coverage', {}).get('verified_at_sha') or current_code_sha()` (or source from wherever the orchestrator stamps it) to `build_payload`'s return dict, as an optional field on the existing `plan_review_result_v2` schema (no version bump needed if the schema guard accepts both present and absent). Name the field explicitly in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the sidecar payload must also persist the registry version stamp (e.g. as `regver`) alongside `verified_at_sha`, and define how `registry_unchanged` is evaluated on the sidecar-fallback path.
evidence: Current code (attest.py line 252): `reasons['registry_unchanged'] = manifest_regver(manifest) == registry_version(repo_root)` — reads `regver` from the SIGNED manifest
sidecar.build_payload returns dict with no `regver` or `registry_version` field (confirmed by full function read)
Plan text describes four rekey preconditions but only explicitly says 'persist the review-time code SHA' — regver is conspicuously absent from the 'persist ... if not already present' clause
ABSENCE: no mention of adding `regver` to the sidecar payload anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The `registry_unchanged` precondition on the sidecar fallback path has no corresponding sidecar field to read from. The current implementation reads `manifest_regver(manifest)` from the signed manifest. For the new sidecar path, `sidecar.build_payload` (confirmed) does not persist the registry version stamp (`regver`). The plan never mentions adding it.
findings.location: ## What — sidecar fallback preconditions; attest.py::remediation_mode_candidate lines 217, 252; sidecar.py::build_payload
scenarios: Implementer has no sidecar field to compare against `registry_version(repo_root)` on the sidecar path; must either skip the `registry_unchanged` check (weakening the gate) or invent a field name.

Without regver in the sidecar, a criteria-registry change between round 1 and round 2 of a BLOCK loop would incorrectly set `registry_unchanged=True` (no comparison target → defaults to some value), making the floor eligible when it should not be.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'regver': registry_version(repo_root)` to `sidecar.build_payload` (pass `repo_root` or stamp it in `emit`), and in the sidecar-path branch of `remediation_mode_candidate` compare `sidecar_payload.get('regver') == registry_version(repo_root)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a decision-rule specification to the plan (or to the function docstring) stating the exact branching: e.g. 'try signature first; if no valid plan-review manifest found, fall through to the sidecar path; `reasons["signed"]` is True only on the signature path,' with explicit handling of the 'stale signature + valid sidecar' case.
evidence: Plan text: 'The signature-based path remains valid when present (a signed baseline is still the strongest reference); the sidecar path is the fallback that makes BLOCK loops eligible.'

Current code (attest.py lines 228–233): `result = signing.verify_signature(...); manifest = ... if result.get('verified') else None; if not is_plan_review_manifest(manifest): return {'eligible': False, 'reasons': reasons}` — this is the exact early-return the new code must NOT take for BLOCK loops
ABSENCE: no pseudocode, no statement of whether `reasons['signed']` is set True/False/removed on the sidecar path, no specification of what triggers the sidecar branch
findings.finding: The control-flow / fallback protocol between the signature-based path and the sidecar-based fallback path inside `remediation_mode_candidate` is unspecified. The plan says 'signature-based path remains valid when present; sidecar path is the fallback,' but never defines the branching logic: the current function early-returns `eligible=False` when `not is_plan_review_manifest(manifest)`. A BLOCK loop has no manifest, so the new code must try the sidecar path instead — but the exact decision rule (when to fall through, whether both paths ever run, what `reasons['signed']` means on the sidecar path) is not stated.
findings.location: ## What — 'The signature-based path remains valid when present (a signed baseline is still the strongest reference); the sidecar path is the fallback'; attest.py::remediation_mode_candidate lines 228–233
scenarios: Implementer must guess whether to use if/else, try-sidecar-first, or a sequential try-signature-then-sidecar structure.
A BLOCK loop with a valid-but-stale plan-review signature (e.g. from a prior PASS then material edit) may ambiguously fall into the signature path or the sidecar path depending on implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name the new sidecar field (e.g. `code_sha`) in the What/Scope section and add it to the `build_payload` spec, including what value it takes (output of `current_code_sha()` at emit time, or `None` in local/unsigned mode), and how `latest_review_result`/the eligibility reader will surface it.
evidence: sidecar.py `build_payload` (lines 428-455): top-level fields are schema, impact_model_version, verdict, ticket_id, ticket_type, material_fingerprint, model, runner, metrics, coverage, findings, coaching — no code SHA field
Plan phrase: 'material_fingerprint is already persisted' (confirmed), 'reviewed-code SHA come from the most recent sidecar' (requires adding the field)
AC #1: 'no certified signature but a prior sidecar … code + registry unchanged' — requires the sidecar to carry both material fingerprint AND a code SHA
findings.finding: The plan states 'material_fingerprint is already persisted; persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present' — but the current `build_payload` in `sidecar.py` does not write `verified_at_sha` or any code-SHA field. The 'if not already present' qualifier is therefore never true: the field must be added. The plan's What section treats this as a minor conditional but it is a required implementation step whose absence would make AC #1 and AC #3 unachievable (no code SHA in the sidecar → `code_unchanged` cannot be evaluated on the sidecar path).
findings.location: What — 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present'
scenarios: Story is implemented without adding the code SHA to `build_payload`; `code_unchanged` in the sidecar path always returns False (no SHA to compare) — BLOCK loops remain ineligible, defeating the story's purpose
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `build_payload`'s return dict: `"code_sha": (verdict.get("coverage") or {}).get("verified_at_sha")` (if the orchestrator already threads this through coverage) or call `current_code_sha()` at emit time. Name the field in the plan's What section explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC4 (or add a sibling AC) with a positive oracle verifying the replacement paragraph is present and describes the sidecar-based eligibility path, not just that the old 'certified' language is absent.
evidence: Scope: 'replacing the honest-limitation paragraph added by the docs child of this epic'
AC4 oracle: `grep -n "certified" docs/plan-review-gate.md` shows no claim — this only checks removal
No AC checks that a correct replacement paragraph describing sidecar-based eligibility is present
findings.finding: AC4 verifies only the *removal* of the old docs claim (grep for 'certified' returning no hit) but the Scope says the paragraph is *replaced* — the replacement content (correct new eligibility description) is described work with no oracle verifying it was actually written.
findings.location: AC4 ('grep -n "certified" docs/plan-review-gate.md shows no claim that eligibility requires a certified signature') vs Scope ('docs update in docs/plan-review-gate.md (replacing the honest-limitation paragraph)')
scenarios: An implementer deletes the paragraph entirely (or replaces it with a stub) — AC4's grep oracle passes, but the docs are now incomplete or misleading about the new eligibility behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC4: 'AND `grep -n "sidecar" docs/plan-review-gate.md` returns at least one line describing the sidecar-based eligibility path.' Or add AC6: 'docs/plan-review-gate.md contains a paragraph describing prior-sidecar eligibility; oracle: grep for a key term from the replacement paragraph.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC4's oracle to assert the positive claim: e.g., `grep -n 'sidecar' docs/plan-review-gate.md` returns a match AND `grep -n 'certified\|requires.*sign' docs/plan-review-gate.md` returns no eligibility-gating claim, or add a brief description of the updated paragraph's required content so a reviewer can verify correctness.
evidence: grep -n 'certified' only checks for the exact token 'certified'
The docs could retain phrases like 'requires a prior signed review' or 'depends on a valid signature' that convey the same constraint the plan intends to remove, yet the grep passes
VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY item (1): substring/token false-negative — absence of one word does not prove the paragraph's meaning changed
findings.finding: AC4's proving command (`grep -n "certified" docs/plan-review-gate.md` showing no results) verifies only the absence of one specific word, not that the eligibility description is correct — the docs could use 'signed', 'signature', or equivalent language to convey the same outdated requirement while passing the grep.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: Oracle: `grep -n "certified" docs/plan-review-gate.md` shows no claim that eligibility requires a certified signature.
scenarios: Executor updates the paragraph to say 'requires a prior signed review' (removing 'certified') — grep passes but docs still mislead users about the sidecar-based eligibility path.
findings.suggested_fix: AC4 oracle: `grep -nE 'sidecar|prior_review' docs/plan-review-gate.md` returns ≥1 match (sidecar path documented) AND `grep -nE 'eligib.*certif|certif.*eligib|require.*sign' docs/plan-review-gate.md` returns 0 matches.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the integrity story for the sidecar-sourced code SHA: either (a) treat the sidecar path as strictly best-effort and document that `code_unchanged` on the sidecar path is advisory (a compromise vs. the signed path), or (b) describe a mechanism (e.g. cross-checking against `current_code_sha()` rather than a stored SHA, or requiring the sidecar value match the currently-observed SHA) that doesn't rely on stored sidecar integrity.
evidence: sidecar.py `build_payload` does not write `verified_at_sha` or any code SHA field — the plan's 'if not already present' qualifier is moot; it must be added
sidecar.py module docstring: 'reducer-ignored sidecar', 'best-effort', no integrity protection described
attest.py line 246-248: existing `code_unchanged` reads `verified_at_sha_from_manifest(manifest)` from the HMAC/opcert-covered signed manifest — a cryptographically authenticated source
Plan 'What': 'baseline material fingerprint and reviewed-code SHA come from the most recent sidecar' — no mention of how sidecar integrity is assured

Plan 'What': 'The signature-based path remains valid when present' — but for BLOCK loops the signed path is always absent, so the unauthenticated sidecar IS the operative path for the 76%-of-cases scenario the plan targets
findings.finding: The sidecar-fallback path for `code_unchanged` reads `verified_at_sha` from an unsigned, best-effort `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar (which currently doesn't even persist the code SHA), while the existing signed-path reads this same value from the cryptographically-protected manifest. The plan introduces no integrity mechanism for the sidecar-sourced code SHA, meaning an adversarial or accidental sidecar mutation could make a drifted-code re-review appear eligible for the rising floor. This is a novel trust-model downgrade on a security-relevant precondition that the plan does not address.
findings.location: What / Scope — 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present'; AC #2 — 'code drifted since the prior sidecar, eligibility is FALSE'
scenarios: A sidecar JSON file is corrupted (mid-write crash, git conflict, manual edit) and records a stale code SHA; the sidecar-based `code_unchanged` fires True even though code drifted — the floor applies when it should not

In a CI/attested context where the tickets branch is writable by multiple actors, an attacker edits the sidecar's (future) code-SHA field to match the current SHA, making a stale-code re-review eligible for the floor
findings.suggested_fix: For `code_unchanged` on the sidecar path, compare `current_code_sha()` against the sidecar's persisted code SHA but also validate with a live check: if `current_code_sha()` is available, `code_unchanged = (sidecar_sha is not None and sidecar_sha == current_code_sha())`. This is the same comparison the signed path uses, but substituting the sidecar-stored SHA for the manifest-signed one — the unauthenticated risk then degrades gracefully because a tampered sidecar SHA would still be caught whenever the current live SHA disagrees.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full `coverage.remediation` sub-schema (the complete set of `reasons` keys, including how `signed` behaves on the sidecar path vs. the signature path) in the plan or in the `remediation_mode_candidate` docstring, so AC3's oracle and the unit-test fixture can be written against a stable contract.
evidence: AC3 oracle: 'integration test asserting coverage.remediation.eligible is true in the round-2 sidecar'
Code (__init__.py line 518): `verdict.setdefault('coverage', {})['remediation'] = remediation` — writes the full `remediation_mode_candidate` return value
sidecar.build_payload (line 439): `'coverage': verdict.get('coverage', {})` — verbatim passthrough, so coverage.remediation appears as-is
The `reasons` dict currently has keys: signed, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, prior_sidecar, within_window — none of this is defined in the plan for the post-rekey shape
findings.finding: The `coverage.remediation` sub-schema — whose `.eligible` field AC3's oracle asserts against — is referenced but never defined. The existing code writes `verdict['coverage']['remediation'] = remediation` (the full `reasons` dict from `remediation_mode_candidate`). AC3 checks `coverage.remediation.eligible is true in the round-2 sidecar`, but the sidecar's `build_payload` writes `coverage` verbatim from the verdict — so the shape of `coverage.remediation` propagates directly. With the new dual-path logic adding new `reasons` keys (or changing existing ones), the sub-schema is unspecified.
findings.location: AC3 — 'asserting coverage.remediation.eligible is true in the round-2 sidecar'; __init__.py lines 517–518
scenarios: A test asserting `coverage.remediation.eligible` passes today but `coverage.remediation.reasons` has changed shape (new keys, removed 'signed') — the schema is silently different from what other consumers expect.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC1/AC2) that verifies the review-time code SHA is present in the written sidecar artifact after a review run, not only that the eligibility function reads it from a fixture.
evidence: 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present' — What section
AC1 and AC2 implicitly depend on this field but neither verifies it is persisted

findings.finding: The described deliverable 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside material_fingerprint (if not already present)' is a sidecar schema change with no covering acceptance criterion that verifies the field is actually written to and readable from the sidecar output.

Oracle: assert field presence in the round-2 sidecar fixture in the integration test, or via a dedicated unit test that calls the sidecar-write path and reads back the persisted field.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test AC covering the 'sidecar present but code_sha field absent' (v1/legacy sidecar) case, asserting a defined fallback behavior (e.g., ineligible-with-graceful-handling rather than exception).
evidence: AC1 only tests the fully-populated sidecar (plan changed, code+registry unchanged, within window → TRUE).
AC2 only tests code-drift → FALSE.
The plan itself acknowledges 'if not already present' for code SHA, implying v1 sidecars in the wild lack this field.
No AC covers: sidecar present but code_sha field absent (e.g. sidecar written before this change was deployed).
findings.finding: No test AC covers the boundary case of a v1/legacy sidecar that exists but is missing the new code-SHA field, leaving the 'sidecar present but incomplete' transition scenario unguarded.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC1/AC2 (unit tests for remediation_mode_candidate); 'What' section: 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present'
scenarios: A plan that was reviewed before this story shipped has a v1 sidecar without code_sha; on re-review the new eligibility path reads the sidecar, finds no code_sha, and either crashes, silently returns ineligible, or incorrectly returns eligible — none of these outcomes are tested.
Rollback scenario: a sidecar written by the new code is read by old code lacking the field.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] With a prior sidecar that lacks the code_sha field (v1 sidecar), remediation eligibility is FALSE and no exception is raised. Oracle: unit test fixture with a sidecar missing code_sha; assert eligible=False and no AttributeError/KeyError.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py` (specifically `test_no_signature_not_eligible` and related signature-gated negative cases) to the Scope's IN list for modification, noting these tests must be split or replaced to reflect the new two-path (signature OR sidecar) eligibility model.
evidence: test_no_signature_not_eligible at line 84 asserts d['eligible'] is False when no signature is present.

These two assertions are mutually exclusive — the existing test will fail under the new behavior.
The Scope section names only 'unit tests over the eligibility decision matrix' without specifying that existing negative-case tests must be updated.
findings.finding: Existing test `test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py::test_no_signature_not_eligible` is an unenumerated consumer whose behavior directly contradicts the new eligibility semantics and will fail post-change. The plan introduces a sidecar fallback making no-signature eligible, but the test file is not named in scope for modification.
findings.location: Scope section / tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py:84
scenarios: CI runs after the attest.py change; test_no_signature_not_eligible fails because the sidecar fallback now makes the no-signature case eligible, breaking the `make test` AC5 green requirement.
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope, add: 'update existing tests in test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py that assert signed=False→not-eligible, splitting them into: (a) no-signature AND no-sidecar → not eligible, (b) no-signature WITH valid sidecar → eligible (new AC1 case).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State definitively whether the review-time code SHA field must be added to the sidecar schema post-epic (e.g., 'add `review_code_sha` field to the sidecar if the post-epic schema does not already include it; if it does, reference it by its post-epic name X').

findings.finding: The What section conditions the sidecar field addition on investigation: 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present' — this defers the design decision of whether the field must be added to the executor's runtime discovery rather than stating a definitive directive.
findings.location: What section — 'baseline material fingerprint and reviewed-code SHA come from the most recent sidecar (material_fingerprint is already persisted; persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present)'
scenarios: Executor spends time investigating whether the field exists, may reach a different conclusion than the author intended, or may omit the field if it appears superficially present but stores a different SHA format.

findings.finding: AC3's oracle describes what to assert but provides no runnable proving command, unlike AC1/AC2 which both supply explicit pytest invocations.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'A BLOCK-loop integration test (review -> BLOCK -> plan edit -> re-review) reaches the floor path (eligibility TRUE on round 2) without any signature present. Oracle: integration test asserting coverage.remediation.eligible is true in the round-2 sidecar.'
scenarios: An executor writes the integration test but an agent reviewer cannot verify it was run; CI may not include integration tests by default; the test may be placed in a location not covered by `make test`.
findings.suggested_fix: Oracle: `python -m pytest tests/integration -k 'block_loop_remediation' -x` and confirm this path is included in `make test` (or add `make test-integration` to the AC5 command chain).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback/back-out description to the plan — either a feature flag gating the sidecar eligibility path, or an explicit statement that reverting the eligibility function to the signature-only check is the back-out, with the commit/PR boundary that makes that revert safe.
evidence: 'BLOCK loops qualify' — title and Why section
'engaging it [the floor] there is real relief' — Why section acknowledges this is a behavioral change to the gate
No mention of a feature flag, rollback procedure, or expand-contract sequence anywhere in the plan
The SEQUENCING note records a forward dependency (ba33 must complete) but no back-out path if this story's change needs to be reverted
findings.finding: The plan changes a gate mechanism's eligibility behavior (BLOCK-loop plans go from ineligible to eligible for advisory-floor drops) with no described rollback path, feature flag, or expand-contract sequence if the new behavior causes unexpected advisory suppression in production.
findings.location: What section (eligibility rekey), SEQUENCING note, and AC3 (BLOCK-loop integration test)
scenarios: The new eligibility logic triggers the floor in BLOCK loops and drops advisories that should have remained (e.g., a novelty-scorer edge case), requiring rapid revert — but no rollback path is specified, so reverting requires an unplanned manual operation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to What or Scope: 'Back-out: reverting this story's changes to attest.py/manifest.py restores signature-only eligibility; no data migration is needed since the sidecar field addition is additive. Optionally gate the sidecar path behind a config flag for staged rollout.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or a clearly-labelled design-rationale section (in docs/plan-review-gate.md or a dedicated docs/adr/ file) capturing: why the sidecar path is an acceptable trust anchor, what weaker guarantee it carries vs. a certified signature, and any threat-model or rollback considerations.
evidence: 'The signature-based path remains valid when present (a signed baseline is still the strongest reference); the sidecar path is the fallback that makes BLOCK loops eligible.'
Scope says only: 'docs update in docs/plan-review-gate.md (replacing the honest-limitation paragraph)'
No mention of ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc capturing the trust-model rationale, threat model, or rollback considerations for the sidecar path.
ABSENCE: no ADR or design decision record is named anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a two-path trust model for rising-floor eligibility (cryptographic signature as primary, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar as fallback) — a novel architectural/security decision — but captures it only in a user-facing docs update (plan-review-gate.md), with no ADR or design-note recording the rationale, threat model, and trust assumptions of the sidecar path.
findings.location: ## What (two-path eligibility description) / ## Scope (docs update) / AC4
scenarios: A future maintainer weakens or removes the signature path without understanding that sidecar-only carries a weaker trust guarantee — the rationale for the dual-path hierarchy is lost.
A security review of the gate mechanism finds no authoritative record of why a mutable sidecar was accepted as a trust anchor.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR (e.g., docs/adr/0NNN-rising-floor-sidecar-eligibility.md) or a 'Design rationale' subsection in docs/plan-review-gate.md that records: (1) the two-path hierarchy and why it is safe, (2) what the sidecar path cannot guarantee that a signature can, and (3) when/why the stronger path should be preferred. Reference this from the AC4 grep check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the code-SHA field write uses an atomic/idempotent mechanism (e.g., write-to-temp-then-rename, or a conditional single-pass update) and define concurrency ownership so that concurrent review processes cannot corrupt the sidecar.
evidence: 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present' — explicit check-then-act language
No mention of atomic write (e.g., write-to-temp-then-rename), locking, or CAS for the sidecar field addition
No concurrency ownership (which process owns the write, what happens on contention) is specified
findings.finding: The plan specifies adding a new field (review-time code SHA) to the sidecar with the phrase 'if not already present', which is a check-then-act (TOCTOU) pattern. Under concurrent review processes writing to the same sidecar, this races: both processes may read 'field absent', both attempt to write, and the second write may corrupt or silently overwrite the first, or the sidecar may be left in a partially written state.
findings.location: What section — 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present'
scenarios: Two parallel review invocations on the same plan (e.g., CI retry + manual trigger) both see the field absent and both write, leaving a race window
A review process crashes between reading 'absent' and completing the write, leaving a partial sidecar that a subsequent read interprets as malformed or missing the field
findings.suggested_fix: Use a write-to-temp-then-atomic-rename pattern for the sidecar update (standard on POSIX filesystems); document that the writing process is the same one that produces the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, making it single-writer by construction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement (structured log line or metric increment) to the eligibility decision in `remediation_mode_candidate` (or its successor) that records which path was taken (signature / sidecar / ineligible) and the specific failing precondition when eligibility is FALSE.
evidence: No mention of logging, metrics, or tracing in the What, Scope, or AC sections
AC only asserts boolean eligible values via unit/integration tests; no AC requires a log/metric for the eligibility decision
Post-flip field data shows 287/382 ineligible — operators currently have no way to distinguish the new sidecar-absent case from code-drift or registry-change without digging into internals
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new eligibility code path (sidecar-based vs. signature-based) with no observability specification: no log lines, structured fields, metrics, or alerts are called out to indicate which path was taken, why eligibility was FALSE (missing sidecar vs. code drift vs. registry change vs. stale window), or when the new sidecar field is written vs. absent.
findings.location: What section and Acceptance Criteria — eligibility logic, no observability mentioned
scenarios: A BLOCK loop stays ineligible after the change ships; an operator cannot determine whether the sidecar is missing (bug), the code SHA drifted (correct behavior), or the window expired (correct behavior) without adding instrumentation post-hoc
The sidecar-path fallback is silently never taken in production (e.g., sidecar not being written) and the feature appears to work in tests but not in the field

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py::build_payload` (specifically its `build_payload` function) to the Scope's IN list as an edit target, noting it must persist `verified_at_sha` / the review-time code SHA alongside `material_fingerprint`.
evidence: Plan says: 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present' but names no specific function or file location for the persistence write.
sidecar.py build_payload at lines 428-454 does not include verified_at_sha or any code SHA field currently.
The sidecar-fallback eligibility path needs to read the SHA from somewhere — the sidecar is the only option without a signed manifest, requiring build_payload to be updated.
findings.finding: sidecar.py::build_payload is an unnamed edit target. The plan requires persisting the review-time code SHA in the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar so the sidecar-fallback path can source `code_unchanged` without a signed manifest, but `build_payload` (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py) is not named in the plan's Scope or What section. Implementers have no explicit pointer to this function as requiring modification.
findings.location: What section / Scope section
scenarios: Implementer adds the sidecar fallback to attest.py but forgets to update build_payload, leaving the sidecar SHA field missing — the new code_unchanged check reads None and denies eligibility.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, expand the IN list to: 'eligibility rekey (attest.py::remediation_mode_candidate), sidecar field addition in sidecar.py::build_payload (adding verified_at_sha), docs update, unit tests.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the What/AC that the sidecar eligibility reader must treat a missing code-SHA field as `code_unchanged=False` (conservative fallback), and assert the post-rollback schema state: existing sidecar files with the new field are harmlessly ignored by rolled-back code because the field is read with `.get()` and a safe default.
evidence: Plan text: 'persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present' — adds a new JSON field to `build_payload` in sidecar.py
Existing `latest_review_result` reader (sidecar.py:118) has schema guards for v1/v2 but no documented handling for the new field being absent on an old v2 sidecar
Post-rollback: REVIEW_RESULT files are reducer-ignored and never rewritten (sidecar.py:6-14 — 'compaction PRESERVES it'); rolled-back code would encounter files with the new field already present
T11 POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE criterion: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0'

DATA LOSS RISK: not applicable (observability-only, no authoritative state), but an eligibility bug from a missing-field read error could silently deny remediation mode rather than producing data loss
findings.finding: The plan adds a new `verified_at_sha` (review-time code SHA) field to the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar but does not specify: (1) how the eligibility reader handles pre-story sidecars that were written without the field (missing key → should degrade safely to `code_unchanged=False`, not raise or produce an incorrect True); and (2) what the schema state is AFTER rollback — sidecars already written with the new field will persist on disk indefinitely (sidecar files are never rewritten), and the plan does not assert that the rolled-back reader handles them without error.
findings.location: What section ('persist the review-time code SHA alongside it if not already present') and AC — no AC covers reader backward/forward compat or post-rollback sidecar state
scenarios: A ticket has a sidecar written by the new code (with the code-SHA field); the story is rolled back; the old eligibility reader encounters the unexpected field — this is safe only if the reader uses `.get()` and defaults, which the plan never specifies.

Round 2 re-review on a ticket whose most-recent sidecar was written before this story (field absent) — eligibility reader must not crash or produce a wrong `code_unchanged` result; the plan does not specify the fallback value.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'With a sidecar present but missing the code-SHA field (pre-story sidecar), `code_unchanged` is False and eligibility is False (conservative fallback, not an error).' In the What section, add: 'Post-rollback, code that does not write the new field reads old sidecars that contain it via .get(..., None), defaulting code_unchanged=False — no data loss, no error.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
